raw_text:
G. Stégen, 'Le livre I de l'Enéide (Namur 1975).
Bernstein studies an interesting museum piece. Collard's sympathetic profile sheds
light on
an exceptionally
fine editor of Vergil's texts. Perret's
edition and translation of Aeneid 1-8 lacks lustre and falters alongside Geymonat's and Mynors' editions. Stégen offers a school text, as does Muir. The
latter helps to enrich the growing Cambridge Latin Texts which have invaded
the Secondary Schools of the English-speaking world.

R. O. A. M. Lyne (ed.), Ciris. A poem attributed to Vergil. 'Cambridge
VERGILIAN BIBLIOGRAPHY (1977-78)
The present survey of Vergilian scholarship is the sixteenth inthe Vergilius
series. Classical World 68.1 (1974), published in association with The Vergilian Society, Inc., contains my 'Recent Work on Vergil: A Bibliographical
Survey, 1964-1973.' The 92-page
survey
may be ordered either from the
offices of The Classical World or from the Treasurer of The Vergilian Society,
Inc. The same survey has been reprinted, along with earlier surveys compiled
by George E. Duckworth, in The Classical World Bibliography of Vergil (ed.
Walter Donlan), Garland Publishing, Inc., New York & London, 1978. Subsequent surveys have appeared in Vergilius 19 (1973), 20 (1974), 21 1975), 22
(1976), and 23 (1977).
The editor of 'Vergilian Bibliography' welcomes reprints of articles and
bibliographical
notices for inclusion in the annual
review. Kindly address
correspondence to Professor A. G. McKay, Department of Classics, McMaster
University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4M2.

raw_text:
N. Zorzetti, 'L'ironia della differenza. A proposito di Catull. 4 e Catai.
10; AFLN 15 (1972-73) 29-54.
Lyne's new edition of Ciris isexemplary. The introduction and commentary provide a complete account of the poem: itsmanuscripts, style and
language,
poetic
treatment of the
myths,
narrative
style
technique and
method of composition. Lyne argues against Vergilian authorship and proposes a much later date of composition than has hitherto been supposed.
Naumann grapples gamely
with Catalepton
5 and 8, re-examines the Epicurean associations,
but reaches no sure conclusion
regarding authorship.
Zorzetti, who accepts Vergilian authorship for Catalepton 10, regards itas a
satirical reflection on Catullus 4.

The introduction and commentary provide a complete account of the poem: itsmanuscripts, style and language, poetic treatment of the myths, narrative style technique and method of composition. Lyne argues against Vergilian authorship and proposes a much later date of composition than has hitherto been supposed. Naumann grapples gamely with Catalepton 5 and 8, re-examines the Epicurean associations, but reaches no sure conclusion regarding authorship. Zorzetti, who accepts Vergilian authorship for Catalepton 10, regards itas a satirical reflection on Catullus 4

The element of balance and contrast which characterisation provides accents alternative viewpoints on poetry and life. Muecke shows that the programmatic prologues of E. 1, 4, 6 and 10 combine allusions to Theo- 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr
publication_block: 2026 18:14:59 UTC 64 cri tus with Vergil's own innovations inthe genre. Solodow argues that the last three Eclogues are self-consciously concerned with poetry; Eclogue 8 implies the power of poetry; Eclogues 9 and 10, indifferent ways, affirm its importance. The pastoral refuge is not merely powerless and empty; it is also unsafe (771)

Vergilius 23 (1977) 17-20.
Hardie assigns Bucolic I to 40 BC. Maecenas and Octavian supplanted
Pollio after Bucolic 8 which Hardie dates accordingly to October 39 BC.
Moore-Blunt accents Vergil's attention to the techniques of his poetic models,
most notably Theocritus (Idyll 11). Tracy underscores similarities and differences between Idyll 5 and Eclogue 3. The close of the poem issurprising in
light of the heavy indebtedness in form and substance elsewhere to Theocritus. The ending comes with the force of anti-climax and continues the
mood of conflict and tension begun inEclogue I. Berg's review of Monteleone
isvaluable for students of the textual tradition of the Messianic Eclogue. Du
Quesnay's article offers a new interpretation of Eclogue IV that attempts to
account for all the various elements in the poem. He dates the poem September 40 BC., and associates itwith the wedding of M. Antonius and Octavia. The expected child and the new age of concord are foreseen as outcomes
64
cri tus with Vergil's own innovations inthe genre. Solodow argues that the last
three Eclogues are self-consciously concerned with poetry; Eclogue 8 implies
the power of poetry; Eclogues 9 and 10, indifferent ways, affirm its importance. The pastoral
refuge is not merely powerless and empty; it is also
unsafe (771).

raw_text:
Stephen T. Kelly, 'The Gallus Quotation inVergil's Tenth Eclogue,'
Vergilius 23 (1977) 17-20.
Hardie assigns Bucolic I to 40 BC. Maecenas and Octavian supplanted
Pollio after Bucolic 8 which Hardie dates accordingly to October 39 BC.
Moore-Blunt accents Vergil's attention to the techniques of his poetic models,
most notably Theocritus (Idyll 11). Tracy underscores similarities and differences between Idyll 5 and Eclogue 3. The close of the poem issurprising in
light of the heavy indebtedness in form and substance elsewhere to Theocritus. The ending comes with the force of anti-climax and continues the
mood of conflict and tension begun inEclogue I. Berg's review of Monteleone
isvaluable for students of the textual tradition of the Messianic Eclogue. Du
Quesnay's article offers a new interpretation of Eclogue IV that attempts to
account for all the various elements in the poem. He dates the poem September 40 BC., and associates itwith the wedding of M. Antonius and Octavia. The expected child and the new age of concord are foreseen as outcomes
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:14:59 UTC
65
of the marriage. Horace, Epode 16, responds to Eclogue IV after the Brundisium Peace proved fleeting. The models are Theocritus, Idyll 17 and Catullus 64, and Vergil's poem is analysed according to the conventions of basilikon and epithalamium. Stylistic similarities of Theocritean poems, Catullus
64 and the Sibylline Oracles impress a unified tone and stylistic consistency
on the Vergilian bucolic. Du Quesnay's essay is an extremely helpful study of
the historical and literary background of this vexatious poem. Segal advances
from E.
4, 42-4, and E.
3, 94-5
to argue that Vergil contrived a subtle
comparison of bucolic and Utopian details between the two ■^Eclogues. «VergiI
signals a progression from small to great themes, from lowly pastoral realism
to one of man's loftiest and most persistent myths. Kelly examines Eclogue
10, 46-9 and finds points of style and tone which mark the lines as a unit.
Habitual usages, stylistics and inner unity suggest that vv 46-9 were quoted
from Gallus.

raw_text:
K. D. White, Country Life in Classical Times (Ithaca, New York 1977).
Faulkner's thesis highlights Vergil's poetic indebtedness to Lucretius and
his philosophical independence. Johnston's doctoral dissertation shows how
the concept of a golden age evolves inVergil's works, from a 'metallic' age,
based on freedom from toil, to an 'agricultural' age, which becomes 'golden'
as a result of agricultural toil. The new concept, emergent inthe Georgics, is
confirmed in the epic. Leach shows that Vergil's bees are often placed in
contexts involving the regeneration of men and kingdoms. But the implicit
optimism attaching to the picture of the immortal- bees is undercut: they
become a figure by which
to express regret for a potentially ideal world
whose best promises are thwarted
by
the complexities of history. Vergil
shares with
his hero the desire to withdraw into the bee garden. But the
swarm must advance towards the fatal light and a fame extending to the stars.
Leach repeatedly stresses the inter-relationship of Georgics zndAeneid. K. Ò.
White's anthology of texts and plates assembles evocative passage from Vergil
and other classical authors to shed
light on the countryside and country
living.

raw_text:
K. D. White, Country Life in Classical Times (Ithaca, New York 1977).
Faulkner's thesis highlights Vergil's poetic indebtedness to Lucretius and
his philosophical independence. Johnston's doctoral dissertation shows how
the concept of a golden age evolves inVergil's works, from a 'metallic' age,
based on freedom from toil, to an 'agricultural' age, which becomes 'golden'
as a result of agricultural toil. The new concept, emergent inthe Georgics, is
confirmed in the epic. Leach shows that Vergil's bees are often placed in
contexts involving the regeneration of men and kingdoms. But the implicit
optimism attaching to the picture of the immortal- bees is undercut: they
become a figure by which
to express regret for a potentially ideal world
whose best promises are thwarted
by
the complexities of history. Vergil
shares with
his hero the desire to withdraw into the bee garden. But the
swarm must advance towards the fatal light and a fame extending to the stars.
Leach repeatedly stresses the inter-relationship of Georgics zndAeneid. K. Ò.
White's anthology of texts and plates assembles evocative passage from Vergil
and other classical authors to shed
light on the countryside and country
living.

Johnston's doctoral dissertation shows how the concept of a golden age evolves inVergil's works, from a 'metallic' age, based on freedom from toil, to an 'agricultural' age, which becomes 'golden' as a result of agricultural toil. The new concept, emergent inthe Georgics, is confirmed in the epic. Leach shows that Vergil's bees are often placed in contexts involving the regeneration of men and kingdoms. But the implicit optimism attaching to the picture of the immortal- bees is undercut: they become a figure by which to express regret for a potentially ideal world whose best promises are thwarted by the complexities of history. raw_text:
Patricia A. Johnston, 'Eurydice and Proserpina inthe Georgias,' TAPA
107 (1977) 161-72.
Augello
treats Georgics I, 160-75
and
detects mystical and symbolic
values. Rome's impérium is indebted as much to
the land as to military
successes. There is an imprint of Orphic, certainly Eleusinian, spirit inthe
passage. Clay considers why Vergil interrupts his praises of country life with
personal reflections on poetry and philosophy; a schema of the finale assists
the argument. Gandeva detects imperial implications inthe geographical allusions and some awareness of tribal distinctions. Johnston shows that Vergil
not only changes the myth of Eurydice at the close of Geòrgie IV but also, at
the start, the myth of Proserpina. Instead of being examples of resurrection,
they now fail to return to life. In their place come important new advances in
the technology sustaining human life.
51.
L. Alfonsi Sc C. Vitelli, 'Tra Oriente e Occidente: voci vergiliane,'
Aevum 50 (1976) 85-90.

Patricia A. Johnston, 'Eurydice and Proserpina inthe Georgias,' TAPA
107 (1977) 161-72.
Augello
treats Georgics I, 160-75
and
detects mystical and symbolic
values. Rome's impérium is indebted as much to
the land as to military
successes. There is an imprint of Orphic, certainly Eleusinian, spirit inthe
passage. Clay considers why Vergil interrupts his praises of country life with
personal reflections on poetry and philosophy; a schema of the finale assists
the argument. Gandeva detects imperial implications inthe geographical allusions and some awareness of tribal distinctions. Johnston shows that Vergil
not only changes the myth of Eurydice at the close of Geòrgie IV but also, at
the start, the myth of Proserpina. Instead of being examples of resurrection,
they now fail to return to life. In their place come important new advances in
the technology sustaining human life.

pretation. He is particularly concerned with Vergil's use of two related elements to point some significant development which they frame, with the
second usually, though not necessarily, echoing the first in ring composition
of the simplest kind. There are frames round the whole work on a divine and

intervention seemed purposeful. Klein suggests that Vergil's version of the
Golden Age is closer to Plato's Statesman than to Hesiod's Works and Days.

pretation. He is particularly concerned with Vergil's use of two related elements to point some significant development which they frame, with the
second usually, though not necessarily, echoing the first in ring composition
of the simplest kind. There are frames round the whole work on a divine and
human level. Harrison interprets Aeneid 9. 375 haud temere/sc.esse. est visum
as 'it seemed to be no mere accident' indicating that the moon's (Diana's)
intervention seemed purposeful. Klein suggests that Vergil's version of the
Golden Age is closer to Plato's Statesman than to Hesiod's Works and Days.

M. Gigante, 'Il battello del poeta,' AFLN 15 (1972-3) 23-7.
McKay examines the ekphrasis [Aeneid I, 159-168) inthe light of Pompeian landscape murals and nymphaea, the Venus-huntress figure, and the
Julian colony at Carthage as a Vergilian reference. Romoeuf analyses the
ekphrasis at Aeneid I, 466-93 as something less monumental than literary,
and as prelude to the llioupersis which ensues. Fleck and Kraggerud are both
concerned with the contested Helen episode inAeneid 2, 567-88. Exclusion
of the passage remains a personal prerogative of scholars and editors. Stegen
shows how Vergil prepares the Trojans to receive the horse into the city.
Phillips finds evocations of Juno Lucina (and Pronuba) in Vergil's goddess.
^Vergil's implication
that Juno (through
Iris)
is acting in her character of
Lucina at the close of Aeneid 4 are profoundly ironic. Wlosok's article on the
Dido Tragedy is sound and directional. Christmann and the Kopffs are both
concerned with the Gates of Sleep at the close of Aeneid 6. The latter ismore
instructive and original: Aeneas is not meant to be regarded as 'false' dream
of a deceitful Roman and Augustan glory. However 'unreal' the vision, Aeneas
returns as agent and messenger of the Manes with the mission to found Rome.
Clark provides a background study to the relation between Apollo's temple
and
the Cumaean Sibyl's grotto. He examines testimonia, topography and
text as they relate to the Sibyl's Cave. 'Vergil seems deliberately to have used
ambiguity and uncertainty to cast a poetic unity over the whole scene. He
thereby did with the topography what he does with tradition.' (483). Pöschl
offers new insights into the complexity of the Daedalic temple doors. The
University of Glasgow has published two of the books which the late Professor Fordyce (ob. 1974) had completed inhis larger project of compiling a
commentary on the entire second half of the epic. Peter G. Walsh supplies the
introduction which analyses structural principles, leading themes and controlling purposes evident in both books. The commentary is magisterial and
instructive even for the most jaded readers. MacKay studies Aeneid 7. 187-91
in association
with Silius Italicus, Pun. 8. 439-42, and Ovid, Metam. 14,
68

raw_text:
M. Gigante, 'Il battello del poeta,' AFLN 15 (1972-3) 23-7.
McKay examines the ekphrasis [Aeneid I, 159-168) inthe light of Pompeian landscape murals and nymphaea, the Venus-huntress figure, and the
Julian colony at Carthage as a Vergilian reference. Romoeuf analyses the
ekphrasis at Aeneid I, 466-93 as something less monumental than literary,
and as prelude to the llioupersis which ensues. Fleck and Kraggerud are both
concerned with the contested Helen episode inAeneid 2, 567-88. Exclusion
of the passage remains a personal prerogative of scholars and editors. Stegen
shows how Vergil prepares the Trojans to receive the horse into the city.
Phillips finds evocations of Juno Lucina (and Pronuba) in Vergil's goddess.
^Vergil's implication
that Juno (through
Iris)
is acting in her character of
Lucina at the close of Aeneid 4 are profoundly ironic. Wlosok's article on the
Dido Tragedy is sound and directional. Christmann and the Kopffs are both
concerned with the Gates of Sleep at the close of Aeneid 6. The latter ismore
instructive and original: Aeneas is not meant to be regarded as 'false' dream
of a deceitful Roman and Augustan glory. However 'unreal' the vision, Aeneas
returns as agent and messenger of the Manes with the mission to found Rome.
Clark provides a background study to the relation between Apollo's temple
and
the Cumaean Sibyl's grotto. He examines testimonia, topography and
text as they relate to the Sibyl's Cave. 'Vergil seems deliberately to have used
ambiguity and uncertainty to cast a poetic unity over the whole scene. He
thereby did with the topography what he does with tradition.' (483). Pöschl
offers new insights into the complexity of the Daedalic temple doors. The
University of Glasgow has published two of the books which the late Professor Fordyce (ob. 1974) had completed inhis larger project of compiling a
commentary on the entire second half of the epic. Peter G. Walsh supplies the
introduction which analyses structural principles, leading themes and controlling purposes evident in both books. The commentary is magisterial and
instructive even for the most jaded readers. MacKay studies Aeneid 7. 187-91
in association
with Silius Italicus, Pun. 8. 439-42, and Ovid, Metam. 14,

291-6. Trahman considers the making of Aeneas' shield in Vulcan's forge and
the shield itself. Trahman is especially concerned with 407-53, above all with
the simile (408-13). Hellenistic etiquette and the heroic code favoured by
Alexandrian critics of Homer prevent Vulcan from fashioning Aeneas' armour
himself. The description of the finished artwork and its isolation from the
actual
manufacture again
befits Alexandrian practice. West supports Eichholz's earlier stand: The illustrations on the shield of Aeneas are so presented
that they bring vividly before our eyes illustrations which would be conceivable and effective on a metal shield.' The shield is round (clipeus); the
scenes are deliberately (and suggestively) placed and are much more amenable
to visual
representation
than the episodes in Aeneas' vision in the Underworld. Lennox reviews past conceptions of the
night foray of Nisus and
Euryalus. He is particularly concerned with Nisus' role in the adventure and
finds reason for a more optimistic view of the episode than scholars normally
support.
William
finds Juno's brusque, impatient behaviour at Aeneid 7,
61 8ff a marked deviation from the norm; divinities are usually treated to a
miraculous Türöffnung.
Juno's action
bespeaks
the
fiend
Discordia,
the
Ennian example for Vergil's vengeful goddess.

Burnell shows that the stock Roman heroes that are used as examples of
virtue by orators and poets have qualities very close to those Aeneas has at his
best. But the ideal is only to be found in certain passages, where Vergil
idealizes Aeneas, or when Aeneas struggles fitfully towards the heroic ideal.
Duke examines the Camilla episode (Aeneid 11. 497-900), finds the Thracian
King Harpalycus and his daughter Harpalyce, along with Metabus, found in
Cato's Originest formative factors. The misanthropic outlaw, lonely and embittered, is Vergil's
literary creation and he disappears without comment
when his role as Camilla's father has been fulfilled. De Grummond assesses
the importance of the Diomedes reference inAeneid 1, 94-101. Diomedes is

R. Villers, 'Durus Ulixes,' REL 54 (1977) 214-221.
Burnell shows that the stock Roman heroes that are used as examples of
virtue by orators and poets have qualities very close to those Aeneas has at his
best. But the ideal is only to be found in certain passages, where Vergil
idealizes Aeneas, or when Aeneas struggles fitfully towards the heroic ideal.
Duke examines the Camilla episode (Aeneid 11. 497-900), finds the Thracian
King Harpalycus and his daughter Harpalyce, along with Metabus, found in
Cato's Originest formative factors. The misanthropic outlaw, lonely and embittered, is Vergil's
literary creation and he disappears without comment
when his role as Camilla's father has been fulfilled. De Grummond assesses
the importance of the Diomedes reference inAeneid 1, 94-101. Diomedes is
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:14:59 UTC
70
to be regarded as Aeneas' heroic exemptum rather than Achilles. Farron is
convinced that the epic was not meant to be an unqualified glorification of
Rome and Augustus; Aeneas' actions inAeneid 10. 517-60 contain ambiguous undertones. Jones treats the simile inAeneid 10, 707-18 and argues in
favour of a return
to
the manuscript tradition (contra Scaliger,
Bentley,
Heyne,
Ribbeck, Geymonat,
Mynors and Williams). Jones favours Servius'
interpretation of 717-8 as applicable to Mezentius, scapegoat for the Etruscan
nation, brutalized and separate from mankind. Kopff finds reverberations of
and allusions to the Homeric framework of Penelope's story in the Dido
account. Comparison with Penelope'is helpful inunderstanding Dido's failure.
Köves-Zulauf provides Part I of a major study of Camilla. Miles contends that
the transformation in Aeneas' behaviour and outlook takes place before his
ultimate destiny is revealed to him by Anchises; that itis a consequence of his
own perceptions of the future, and reflects a consolidation of his own sense
of values; and that it has important implications for our interpretation of the
kind of peace which Aeneas and
his spiritual descendants are to achieve.
Villers associates Vergil's Ulysses with the post-Homeric tradition. Vergil emphasizes Ulysses' cruel streak inorder to establish Aeneas as a hero entirely
antithetical to the 'enduring' Ulysses.

Ogilby's second translation of the Vergilian corpus was published in1654
in folio form. Carleton studies the plate accompanying the translation and
associates the heavenly scene of apotheosis (Daphnis) with Charles I; the
translation reinforces the interpretation that Daphnis is aligned with Caesar
70
to be regarded as Aeneas' heroic exemptum rather than Achilles. Farron is
convinced that the epic was not meant to be an unqualified glorification of
Rome and Augustus; Aeneas' actions inAeneid 10. 517-60 contain ambiguous undertones. Jones treats the simile inAeneid 10, 707-18 and argues in
favour of a return
to
the manuscript tradition (contra Scaliger,
Bentley,
Heyne,
Ribbeck, Geymonat,
Mynors and Williams). Jones favours Servius'
interpretation of 717-8 as applicable to Mezentius, scapegoat for the Etruscan
nation, brutalized and separate from mankind. Kopff finds reverberations of
and allusions to the Homeric framework of Penelope's story in the Dido
account. Comparison with Penelope'is helpful inunderstanding Dido's failure.
Köves-Zulauf provides Part I of a major study of Camilla. Miles contends that
the transformation in Aeneas' behaviour and outlook takes place before his
ultimate destiny is revealed to him by Anchises; that itis a consequence of his
own perceptions of the future, and reflects a consolidation of his own sense
of values; and that it has important implications for our interpretation of the
kind of peace which Aeneas and
his spiritual descendants are to achieve.
Villers associates Vergil's Ulysses with the post-Homeric tradition. Vergil emphasizes Ulysses' cruel streak inorder to establish Aeneas as a hero entirely
antithetical to the 'enduring' Ulysses.

Ogilby's second translation of the Vergilian corpus was published in1654
in folio form. Carleton studies the plate accompanying the translation and
associates the heavenly scene of apotheosis (Daphnis) with Charles I; the
translation reinforces the interpretation that Daphnis is aligned with Caesar
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:14:59 UTC
71
and with the executed Charles I. Clark argues that Vergil's inclusion of the
Misenus-episode is a rehandling of tradition which created a special difficulty
for the poet in terms of Cumaean topography, and that Aeneas' visit to
Cumae,
like
that of Misenus
itself,
is a further innovation to tradition.
Coarelli states that the sculptures found in the cave at Sperlonga have no
relation with Vergil's epic. Geyer notes Nero's preference for Trojan War
stories and suggests that the Laocoon sculpture, lodged inthe Domus Aurea,
suited his 'classical' orientation. Goodfellow's thesis collects all Transpadane
references, direct and indirect, in order to reassess the part played by boyhood memories inVergil's poetry. Peculiarities of Transpadane flora, fauna,
agricultural procedures, legends and natural phenomena are included in the
study. Ogilvie's review of Hampe's Sperlonga und Vergil (Mainz 1972) corrects improper arguments and associations. Pearsall and Salter devote Chapter
I (pages 3-24) to Classical Traditions, and include Vergil's Italy in their compass. Säflund assigns the 'Germanicus' statue by Cleômenes to M. Claudius
Marcellus, heroised as Hermes Chthonios, and regards it as a posthumous
portrait inspired by Vergil's poetic vision inAeneld 6. Tilly grapples with the
precise location of Fescennium (cf. Aeneid 7. 695-7) and favours Narce, 10
kilometres south of Cività Castellana over Corchiano. Winner argues that the
Laocoon reconstruction was based on Vergil and Pliny.

raw_text:
H. H. Huxley, 'Prayer in Epic with special reference to Virgil,' Bulletin, Class. Assoc. Pacific North West 8:1 (November 1977) 5-6.
116.
Patricia A. Johnston. 'Vergil's Conception of Saturnus,' CSCA 10
(1977).
117.
P. G. Walsh, 'Spes Romana, spes Christiana,' Prudentia 6 (1974)
33-40.
Clark's book assembles fragments relating to the 'Descent' theme from
Near Eastern, Greek and
Roman
literature and cult to Vergil's time. He
concentrates on the wisdom-tradition (rather than on the vegetation tradition) and emphasizes Vergil's originality inhis use of the theme. Underworld
myths, along with study of their locales, underworld geography, and ritualistic
implications, provide
fresh
insights into Vergil's Aeneid 6. Harrison's
article sheds new light on the doctrine of transmigration of souls which lies at
the heart of Anchises' sermon and Aeneas' vision. Huxley's paper recalls that
Vergil
in his much-abused battle-scenes most effectively examines the way
men petition the gods and inso doing achieves unsurpassed effects of pathos
and
tragic
irony. Johnston's forthcoming article shows how Vergil's conception of Saturnus evolves from a rough equivalent of Hesiod's Cronus inthe
Eclogues, to the semi-historical, Euhemeristic figure described by Evander in
Aeneid 8. Walsh examines the concept of hope with emphasis on Augustan
writers, Apuleius, and Paulinus of Nola.

raw_text:
H. J. Tschiedel, 'Vergil und die römische Liebeselegie/ Lebendige
Literatur. Dialog Schule-Wiss., K/ass. Sprachen und Lit. 10 (München 1977)
120-55.
Evans shows that Ovid, Tristia 3, 10 relies on Vergil, Georgics 3, 349-83.
Vergil's is an instance of the Scythian 'cold' topos, but Ovid's is a serious
description affected by his own experience. Gaisser identifies literary allusion
as an essential component of Tibullus' technique and shows that Tibullus 2.3
is an
imitation of Vergil's Eclogue 10.
But by an ironic juxtaposition of
conventions, pastoral and elegiac, Tibullus acknowledges his imitation of Vergil and asserts his own position as elegist. Perutelli detects rhetorical and
elegiac adaptations of Vergilian themes.

Crabbe is concerned with Vergil's use of the Theseus and Ariadne theme in
the account of the Orpheus and Eurydice. The article examines compositional
technique in the Aristaeus episode as a whole. Sutton provides a conjectural
account of the origin of the Cacus myth. He suggests that the inspiration
rested in Euripides' satyric drama Sisyphus (or something comparable) as

raw_text:
Friedrich Werner, Nachahmung und eigene Gestaltung in der bukolischen Dichtung des Titus Calpurnius Siculus. Diss.,' Frankfort-am-Main, 1976.
Cupaiuolo studies Ànth. Lat. 223 (Riese), a poem of 21 hexameters assigned to an African poet (5-6 c. A.D.). Walsh argues that Vergilian allusions
in Paulinus (ca. 355-431 A.D.), both words and techniques, were 'baptised' to
serve a Christian purpose. The reflections of Vergil are a clear index to the
nature of education inBordeaux and are part of the well-stocked mind of the
Christian writer. Delz refers to Vergil for explication of Silver Age poems.
Pasoli's analysis of Juvenal 3, 315-322 involves a comparison with the close of
Eclogue 1 and Horatian passages. Vessey compares Hannibal's shield (Silius
Italicus, Punica. 2, 395-452) with Aeneas' shield to show how the ekphrasis
objectifies central themes of the epic.

nature of education inBordeaux and are part of the well-stocked mind of the
Christian writer. Delz refers to Vergil for explication of Silver Age poems.
Pasoli's analysis of Juvenal 3, 315-322 involves a comparison with the close of
Eclogue 1 and Horatian passages. Vessey compares Hannibal's shield (Silius
Italicus, Punica. 2, 395-452) with Aeneas' shield to show how the ekphrasis
objectifies central themes of the epic.
title: Diss.,' Frankfort-am-Main
publication_block: 1976. Cupaiuolo studies Ànth. Lat. 223 (Riese), a poem of 21 hexameters assigned to an African poet (5-6 c. A.D.). Walsh argues that Vergilian allusions in Paulinus (ca. 355-431 A.D.), both words and techniques, were 'baptised' to serve a Christian purpose. The reflections of Vergil are a clear index to the nature of education inBordeaux and are part of the well-stocked mind of the Christian writer. Delz refers to Vergil for explication of Silver Age poems. Pasoli's analysis of Juvenal 3, 315-322 involves a comparison with the close of Eclogue 1 and Horatian passages. 76
Cugusi detects evidence of Vergilian quotations from a school manual; the
grammar and prosody are faulty. Currie compares R. S. Thomas' twentiethcentury
poetry
with
Vergil's. Thomas' Wales and Vergil's Campania are
worlds apart but there are common elements: the hortatory tone of an instructor or preacher, and a common religious atmosphere. The strains are
Vergilian
but Thomas has disclaimed Vergilian influence. Edmunds shows
that several of Robert Frost's poems derive from Vergil's Georgias and Eclogues. Garton's study of a 17th century cento on the Resurrection of the
Body shows that the learned cleric drew on Vergil's major opera, and oddly
enough, on Mephaeus Vegius' Supplementum to the Aeneid. Garton provides
a translation along with the Latin mosaic-work of the cento. Jan Kott detects
Vergilian
influence in Shakespeare's Tempest: both poets were concerned
with a tempest-tossed hero, insearch of a New World. Shakespeare was sensitive to the paradigm of the journey by sea and saw Everyman associations in
the journey through penance to salvation and the attainment of the Promised
Land. But Shakespeare not only invoked the Vergilian example, he challenged
and finally rejected it. La Marca's article demonstrates that the funeral games
of lacobo Sannazaro were inspired by Aeneid 5 and Iliad 23. Lee's comparison of Nahum Tate's libretto for Henry Purcell's Dido and Aeneas (1689)
with Vergil's poem sheds light on tastes and standards of both authors and
their ages.
Purcell
wrote
his opera for Josias Priest's Boarding School for
Young Gentlewomen and employed Tate for the libretto. Tate provided a
romanticized
hero and
heroine.
Romano's paper noted that Renaissance
English pastoral was dependent on commentaries provided to illuminate Vergil's frequently baffling text. Two approaches may be detected in the commentaries:
allegorical and rhetorical. Renaissance English commentaries inspired
two versions
of pastoral, those of Spenser, and those of Sidney.
Schleiner shows that Crashaw and Sidney stressed filial piety during the retreat from Troy, whereas late
17th and
18th
century
writers were more
concerned with the domestic pathos attaching to Ascanius and Creusa. Emblem books and engravings support the thesis.
McMaster University
A. G. McKay
